2ccg 4 minutes ago | parent | context | on: Eight years of wanting, three months of building w...

Your whole argument is based on 'the technology improves'.

Ok, so another fundamental proposition is monetary resources are needed to fund said technology improvement.

Whats wrong with LLMs? They require immense monetary resources.

Is that a problem for now? No because lots of private money is flowing in and Google et al have the blessing of their shareholders to pump up the amount of cash flows going into LLM based projects.

Could all this stop? Absolutely, many are already fearing the returns will not come. What happens then? No more huge technology leaps.

nobris 8 minutes ago [dead] | parent | context | on: Loqi, a memory system that preserves context after...

I built Loqi to test a different approach to AI memory.

Most memory systems act like static RAG: store documents, retrieve by similarity when asked.

Loqi is trying a temporal memory model instead: - memory is stored at section level - useful links are built as work happens - consolidation runs between tasks - retrieval uses semantic, trigger, and graph channels - a small local model suppresses bad trigger firings

The strongest current result is a compaction experiment: after context loss, the no-memory system dropped to 17% policy compliance, while Loqi recovered to 63% (+46 percentage points).

There is also an end-to-end local agent loop: - task arrives - Loqi retrieves relevant memory - memory is injected into the actor prompt - episode is logged - consolidation runs between tasks

It is still a research prototype, not a production system, and it does not beat flat RAG overall on every benchmark yet. But it is showing real signal in the places I care about: context loss, semantic confounds, and learned reuse over time.

Repo: https://github.com/wf802222/loqi

tatrions 35 minutes ago [dead] | parent | context | on: Codex pricing to align with API token usage, inste...

This is partially true but overstated. Input tokens are known before the call - you can count them. Output tokens are bounded by max_tokens. So for a single LLM call, cost is actually predictable within a range.

The real unpredictability comes from agentic loops: how many tool calls will the agent make? Will it retry? How deep does the chain go? That's where costs blow up and nobody can give you a number upfront.

But the bigger issue with subscription pricing isn't unpredictability - it's that you're paying the same whether your query needs a frontier model or could be handled by something 50x cheaper. Token-based pricing at least makes that visible. The next logical step is routing queries to the cheapest model that can handle them well, which is a solvable problem even if exact cost prediction isn't.

sdfjkhdfjkdhs 37 minutes ago | parent | context | on: Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

What is a "normie"?
sdfjkhdfjkdhs 39 minutes ago | parent | context | on: Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

> A launch is detectable seismically, visually, on radar, etc.

Does that rocket have an "escape chute", just like the shuttle did, that conveniently allows the "astronauts" to slide down to safety before the rocket launches? I'm betting it does.

> It is many orders of magnitude easier to go to the moon than to convincingly fake it.

Completely wrong. Wasn't true in the 1960s, and isn't true now.

People are easily convinced by lies, as you have demonstrated just now.

We do not, at this point in time, possess the technology to go to the moon. It may be decades before such tech is actually developed.

GandalfHN 42 minutes ago [dead] | parent | context | on: Codex pricing to align with API token usage, inste...

That is too broad. Subsidies around shared infra like NTP or public DNS can cut waste, because once you meter each internal API call and GPU minute, teams start optimizing for chargeback theater and budget blame instead of the product.
sdfjkhdfjkdhs 43 minutes ago | parent | context | on: Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

Correct. This guy gets it. All other replies can be disregarded.
sdfjkhdfjkdhs 44 minutes ago | parent | context | on: Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

Now you're catching on.
sdfjkhdfjkdhs 45 minutes ago | parent | context | on: Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

How to talk to a science cultist: you can't, as your post will be immediately flagged and censored.
tatrions 46 minutes ago [dead] | parent | context | on: Codex pricing to align with API token usage, inste...

this cuts both ways though. providers can't predict cost, but now developers can't either and you get "token anxiety" where you second-guess every prompt before sending it.

the practical workaround is classifying on the client side before you hit the API. even a simple check on whether the prompt has code blocks, multi-file context, or is just a question gives you enough signal to pick the right model tier. you don't need to predict exact token count, just whether this is a sub-cent query or a ten-cent query and route accordingly. makes per-token pricing actually manageable.

sdfjkhdfjkdhs 48 minutes ago | parent | context | on: Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

Nope.
tatrions 53 minutes ago [dead] | parent | context | on: Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code ...

the pay-as-you-go option is honestly better than people think once you stop sending everything to the same model. i moved a coding workflow to API after hitting limits too many times and at first it was brutal on cost. but then i started looking at what each request was actually doing and realized 70-80% of the tokens were going to stuff a much cheaper model handles fine. autocomplete, test scaffolding, simple refactors.. none of that needs opus.

the subscription hides this because everything's bundled. you never think about which requests are wasteful. on API you see it immediately and can start routing by complexity instead of just blasting the frontier model for everything.

54agfvb 54 minutes ago | parent | context | on: Japanese, French and Omani vessels cross Strait of...

darshil2023 1 hour ago [dead] | parent | context | on: Ask HN: Learning resources for building AI agents?

If you enjoyed the Gulli/Bhagwat pattern-based approach, you should look into the "Memory-First" movement.

    Microsoft’s Agent Framework (The AutoGen Evolution): In early 2026, Microsoft officially merged AutoGen and Semantic Kernel. This is now the "standard" for enterprise multi-agent orchestration. It focuses on Event-Driven Architecture, which is more robust than simple DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs).

    PydanticAI: This has become the favorite for "Hacker News" types who value type-safety and structured data. It’s much more "Pythonic" than LangChain and focuses on making agents behave like predictable software rather than black-box prompt wrappers.

    Model Context Protocol (MCP): Anthropic’s protocol is now the industry "USB port" for AI. Learning how to build MCP Servers is the best way to understand how agents actually "touch" the real world (databases, local files, and APIs) in a standardized way.
54agfvb 1 hour ago | parent | context | on: Japanese, French and Omani vessels cross Strait of...
